title: Grand Palace & Wat Phra Kaew
description: The most elaborate complex in the country — a walled city of spires, mirrored mosaic towers, and royal halls that took over two centuries to build. Wat Phra Kaew holds the Emerald Buddha, Thailand's most sacred image. Come early. The heat and crowds are real.

title: Wat Pho — Reclining Buddha
description: A 46-meter reclining Buddha covered in gold leaf, housed in a gallery that barely contains it. The temple grounds are one of the oldest in Bangkok. Stay for the traditional massage school attached to the complex — it's the original location.

title: Muay Thai at Rajadamnern Stadium
description: The oldest and most prestigious Muay Thai venue in Thailand. The crowd is real — gamblers in the upper deck signaling odds with hand gestures, fighters in the ring moving with a precision that doesn't translate through a screen. Rajadamnern is the one to choose over Lumpinee if you want atmosphere.

title: Wat Arun — Temple of Dawn
description: A prang tower crusted in porcelain fragments that catch the light differently at every hour. Best seen at golden hour from the opposite riverbank. Crossable by a short ferry. Climb the steep inner stairs for the Chao Phraya panorama above.

title: Muay Thai Training at Khongsittha — Bangkok
description: The only Bangkok Muay Thai gym certified by the Ministry of Education with a proper visa program. Train with structure and authenticity that tourist sessions can't replicate. Watch real fights at Lumpinee or Rajadamnern stadium at night after training.

title: Wat Paknam Phasi Charoen
description: A 69-meter golden chedi rises over Thonburi, capped by a crystal-clear glass pagoda with 360-degree river views. Beneath it, an unforgettable ceiling mural blends Buddhist cosmology with a night-sky projection — genuinely one of the most photographed ceilings in Bangkok, and still under most tourists' radar. Take the elevator up through five museum floors before reaching the top.

title: Wat Ratchanatdaram Worawihan — The Iron Monastery
description: The only Iron Monastery left standing anywhere in the world — 37 black spires spiraling around a central tower, each named for a virtue on the path to enlightenment. Climb the narrow internal stairs for a rooftop view over Ratchadamnoen and the Golden Mount next door. Fewer crowds than its famous neighbor, same skyline.

title: Chinatown Night Walk — Yaowarat Road
description: Bangkok's Chinatown at night is one of the densest street food corridors in Asia. Grilled seafood, roast duck, mango sticky rice, bird's nest vendors, gold shops open past midnight, and neon signs in three languages. Walk the full length of Yaowarat and Charoen Krung before you eat.

title: National Museum Bangkok
description: The largest museum in Southeast Asia and the single best place to build a foundation in Thai history before traveling anywhere else in the country. Prehistoric tools through the Rattanakosin era — the collection spans every dynasty and covers all the context you'll need.

title: Muay Thai Fight Night — Ringside at Lumpinee or Rajadamnern
description: Real fighters, real stakes. Ringside seats put you inside the ritual: the wai kru pre-fight ceremony, the piphat music that changes tempo with the fight, the gamblers in the stands. A performance art, a sport, and a spiritual practice simultaneously.

title: Snake Farm (Queen Saovabha Memorial Institute)
description: A working research institute and venom extraction center that doubles as Thailand's most unusual tourist site. Live snake handling shows on a schedule. Not for the faint.

title: Chao Phraya River Boat
description: The express boat running up and down the Chao Phraya is one of the best city orientations available anywhere in Southeast Asia. Pass temples, raft houses, colonial warehouses, and the city skyline from the water. Costs almost nothing. No tour bus required.

title: IconSiam — The Supertall Mall
description: A riverside mega-mall that deserves to be experienced on its own terms — part of it is a floating market recreated indoors, with actual boats and real vendors. The food hall is enormous. The AC is aggressive. On a 38-degree afternoon, this is the correct call.

title: Khao San Road Night
description: The most famous backpacker street in the world — chaotic, loud, and completely unapologetic about it. Bucket cocktails, tuk-tuk touts, knockoff gear, and travelers from every country all in four blocks. Worth one night for the spectacle alone.

title: Traditional Thai Massage — Wat Pho School
description: The massage school at Wat Pho is the original — monks practiced here before it became a formal institution. A 1-hour traditional session in the open-air sala. Not a spa. Louder and less refined than most massage places in the city, which is exactly why it's the right one.

title: Lumpini Park — Early Morning
description: The city's central green space is most alive before 8am — tai chi practitioners, outdoor aerobics groups, monitor lizards in the lake, and Bangkok residents doing what they do before the heat arrives. A quiet hour in Lumpini is the best reset the city offers.
